_meta.source: Structured Communication KB for Therapist-Guided Narrative Game (kb2.md)
_meta.purpose: Per-disorder communication rules for voice interview, NPC dialogue, and game design
_meta.rule: The game is a therapeutic communicator, not a therapist

disorders.name: Anxiety Disorders
disorders.communication_landscape: Hyper-attuned to ambiguity. The interaction itself can become a trigger. Ambiguity, unexpected shifts, or pressure to answer 'correctly' can escalate anxiety in real time.
opens_them_up: Offering choices and control at every junction
Pacing: speak slowly, leave room for response, never rush
Normalizing: 'A lot of people find that part challenging'
Small, concrete first steps before emotionally complex quests
helpful_phrases: There's no pressure to go anywhere you don't want to go in this story.
Your character gets to set the pace.
What's the safest part of this world for your character right now?
I noticed your character is hesitating here. What do you think they're feeling?
avoid: Unpredictability — telegraph transitions
Piling on follow-up questions
Validating reassurance-seeking in loops
Timed responses or performance pressure
High-stimulation scenes without a decompression path
npc_design: Companion NPC who models calm affect and regulated behavior
Allow the character to 'prepare' before new challenges — ritual = autonomic regulation
NPCs should validate hesitation: 'Are you sure you want to go in? There's no rush.'

disorders.name: Major Depressive Disorder
disorders.communication_landscape: Anhedonia, psychomotor slowing, negative cognitive triad. Short answers or disengagement is symptom expression, not resistance.
opens_them_up: Behavioral activation via micro-quests — small achievable challenges
Emphasizing meaning over performance
Social connection as game mechanic — NPCs who check in and remember
Progress visibility — narrative journal tracking what character has done
helpful_phrases: Your character has been through a lot. What do they need most right now?
Even small steps count in this world.
I see your character. They matter to this story.
What's one thing your character is still holding on to?
avoid: Performative or forced enthusiasm
Long branching dialogue trees requiring sustained concentration
Narrative arcs that begin with crisis or suffering
Asking 'What makes you happy?' — unanswerable with anhedonia
Closing scenes with unresolved negative affect
npc_design: NPC who has 'been through something hard' and is still showing up
NPCs should notice the character: 'I've been thinking about you.'
NPCs should reach out, not wait to be approached

disorders.name: PTSD and Trauma-Related Disorders
disorders.communication_landscape: Exquisitely sensitive to safety, control, and re-traumatization. Highest-stakes voice interaction.
opens_them_up: Predictability and safety above all else — announce transitions
Explicit user control: 'You decide how much your character shares'
Gradual titration of emotionally loaded content
Externalization via character — 'What happened to your character' not 'what happened to you'
Titrated exposure through narrative
helpful_phrases: Your character gets to decide what they remember and what they keep private.
It takes a lot of courage to keep going in this story. You're doing that.
No matter when something happened to your character, what they're feeling now is real.
Your character can pause here as long as they need.
I'm not going to ask you to explain anything you don't want to.
That sounds like something your character has been carrying for a long time.
avoid: Pressing for trauma narrative details before readiness
Ambush, sudden violence, loss of character control without consent
Physical/spatial metaphors mapping onto trauma ('trapped', 'held down')
Labeling as 'survivors' or 'victims'
Tone suggesting urgency or impatience
Invalidating time: 'it was a long time ago'
Calling them 'brave' or 'resilient' in shame-inducing ways
trauma_informed_language: People-first: 'a person who experienced trauma' not 'a traumatized person'
Non-blaming, non-shaming: language centering agency and dignity
Normalize nervous system responses: 'Your character's body is doing exactly what bodies do'
npc_design: At least one NPC embodying safety — consistent, warm, non-demanding, always there
NPCs never betray trust, surprise, or disappear without explanation
Character has a 'safe place' they can return to at any time
NPC dialogue models co-regulation: calm breathing, grounded responses, witnessing

disorders.name: Substance Use Disorders
disorders.communication_landscape: Shame and ambivalence define communication challenges. Direct confrontation entrenches avoidance.
opens_them_up: Exploring ambivalence: 'What would be different if they didn't need the substance?'
Strengths-based language: what they HAVE managed
Rolling with resistance — validate the part not ready to change
NPC companions modeling managing difficulty without substances
helpful_phrases: Your character has been coping with something really hard. That takes a lot out of you.
What has your character tried when things get really overwhelming?
It sounds like part of them wants something different. What does that part look like?
There's no wrong answer here. We're just exploring.
On a scale of 1-10, how much does your character care about making a change right now?
avoid: Any language conveying moral judgment
Ultimatums or high-pressure NPC dialogue
Game mechanics punishing substance-related choices with shame
Pushing for commitment to change before readiness
Directly confronting denial — arguing with resistance reinforces it
npc_design: NPCs in recovery — honest about own struggles, not preachy
Game world shows natural consequences without moralizing narration
'What matters to you' mechanic built into quest structure

disorders.name: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder
disorders.communication_landscape: Executive dysfunction, history of criticism and failure. Deep shame around underperformance.
opens_them_up: Gamification matches ADHD neurology — immediate feedback, novelty, reward loops
Short interview segments: 2-3 questions max before a game transition
Positive reinforcement of engagement, not performance
Choice architecture: frequent branching decisions
Progress visibility: badges, maps, journal entries
helpful_phrases: What's the most interesting thing your character could do right now?
Great — let's keep moving.
Your character noticed something important there.
Let's take that in a different direction — what about...?
avoid: Long monologue explanations or complex NPC backstory dumps
Multi-step instructions or questions
Drawing attention to tangents or 'going off track'
Frustration cues when user takes non-linear paths
Quest structures with no clear short-term reward cycle
npc_design: NPC embodying ADHD traits positively — creative, spontaneous, inventive
Frequent in-world discoveries rewarding curiosity
Timer-free zones as default; optional urgency clearly labeled

disorders.name: Bipolar Disorder
disorders.communication_landscape: Must adapt to radically different user states: expansive/talkative in hypomania vs withdrawn/brief in depression.
opens_them_up: Naming the weather, not the diagnosis: 'Your character seems really energized today'
Psychoeducation in game lore: world has rhythms mirroring mood cycles
Stability-building: consistent NPCs, predictable rituals, clear structure
IPSRT-aligned mechanics: game rewards routine
helpful_phrases: Your character's energy seems different today. What's the world like from where they're standing?
What does your character need most right now to feel steady?
What's one thing that keeps your character grounded when everything is moving fast?
avoid: During high-energy: matching energy (escalation), grandiosity-reinforcing praise
During depressive: high expectations, 'why isn't your character doing anything?'
Sudden mood-matching — voice assistant maintains calm baseline
Labeling the state: 'It sounds like you might be manic' — therapist's role only
npc_design: Stable NPC anchor whose behavior doesn't mirror mood swings — models co-regulation
Narrative arcs showing cyclical seasons (abundance/scarcity) — universalizing without pathologizing
Safe harbors accessible during any mood state

disorders.name: Schizophrenia Spectrum Disorders
disorders.communication_landscape: Distorted thinking, perceptions, emotions, social engagement challenges. Trust is foundational.
opens_them_up: Simplicity and directness: short sentences, plain language, one idea at a time
Scaffolding: break game elements into very small steps
Validation without confirmation of delusions
Patience: allow long processing pauses
Psychoeducation in NPC dialogue: explain world rules clearly
Manifest interest: 'I'm genuinely interested in what your character thinks'
helpful_phrases: Take your time. There's no rush in this world.
Your character can ask me to explain anything again.
What does your character see from where they're standing?
That sounds like a lot for your character to process. What stands out most?
Your character's thoughts matter in this story. What are they thinking?
avoid: Abstract metaphors, complex symbolism, ambiguous NPC dialogue
Rapid NPC voice changes, multiple simultaneous voices, dissonant audio
Confronting or arguing with unusual perceptions
Rushing transitions or creating narrative disorientation
Jokes or irony without very clear signaling
npc_design: NPCs speak clearly, with one voice, one topic at a time
No whispering NPCs, conspiracies, or talking behind character's back
Central trusted NPC whose loyalty is absolutely consistent

disorders.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
disorders.communication_landscape: Intrusive thoughts and compulsive rituals. Critical trap: reassurance-seeking — answering yes maintains the OCD cycle.
opens_them_up: Normalizing uncertainty as a narrative feature
Externalizing OCD as a character: 'The OCD voice says X — what does the rest say?'
ERP-aligned quests: approach feared situations without checking/avoiding
NPCs who tolerate uncertainty calmly
helpful_phrases: Your character doesn't have to know for sure what's ahead to keep moving.
That uncertain feeling makes sense. What does your character do anyway?
Intrusive thoughts in this world don't predict what will happen — they're just thoughts.
What would your character do if they trusted themselves a little more right now?
avoid: Responding to reassurance-seeking with definitive reassurance
Contamination/checking/catastrophe content without clinical guidance
Punishing compulsive in-game behaviors (induces shame)
Repeating information because asked again — gently redirect instead
npc_design: Companion NPC modeling tolerating uncertainty
NPCs do not compulsively check or verify
Quest rewards tied to completing actions DESPITE uncertainty

disorders.name: Eating Disorders
disorders.communication_landscape: Greatest density of language landmines. Bodies, food, weight, appearance are all live wires. Bypass the disorder's language domain entirely.
opens_them_up: Asking about feelings, not behaviors
Validating complexity without centering food or body
Connecting to values: 'What does your character wish people understood?'
Narrative arcs about nourishment, care, deserving — not about eating
Written disclosure via in-game character journal
helpful_phrases: What's going on for your character emotionally right now?
Your character deserves to take up space in this world.
What does your character need that they haven't been able to ask for?
What would it look like if your character took care of themselves the way they take care of others?
avoid_hard_rules: ANY comment, even positive, about weight, appearance, or body shape
NPC content involving food choices, eating behaviors, body comparisons
Game mechanics involving visual body representation without clinical oversight
Asking about meals, intake, or physical symptoms
npc_design: NPCs are body-neutral by design — no comments on appearance
Food, if present, is purely environmental and non-interactive
Storytelling NPC inviting expression through narrative, art, or music

disorders.name: Borderline Personality Disorder
disorders.communication_landscape: Emotional dysregulation, intense fear of abandonment, unstable self. People with BPD hear words 'inside out, sideways, and devoid of context'. Emotional attunement to feelings, not words.
opens_them_up: Active empathic listening — feelings, not just words
Validation without enabling escalation
Distraction into positive engagement when emotions escalate
Consistent session endings — never abrupt
DBT-informed language: validation, wise mind references
helpful_phrases: Your character's feelings are real, and they matter in this story.
I'm not going anywhere. I'm here for the whole session.
What does the wise part of your character think about this?
Your character is allowed to feel all of this.
Let's give your character a break from this for a moment — there's something interesting just over here...
It makes sense you feel that strongly. What does your character need right now to feel a little safer?
avoid: Sudden session terminations or unannounced interruptions
Arguing with emotional interpretation
Invalidating with logic: 'That doesn't make sense'
Inconsistent NPC behavior, disappearing, expressing rejection
Overstimulating scenes without recovery path
Promises the system cannot keep: 'I will always be here'
npc_design: Most consistently warm, stable NPC as central companion
NPC loyalty and presence never in doubt — no betrayals or unannounced absences
DBT skill metaphors: 'wise elder' for distress tolerance, 'fire keeper' for emotion regulation

gamification_mechanics.clinical_mechanism: Polyvagal safety activation; window of tolerance

gamification_mechanics.mechanic: Narrative arc with ups and downs
